title: Orbital Flight Simulation
unsafe_explanation: This lesson uses scene-level reusable functions with native JavaScript expressions (for loops/closures) to compute trajectory extrema like apogee and perigee from live simulation state. Allow only if you trust this scene source.

scenes.title: Orbit Basics: Speed vs Height
scenes.description: Build intuition for circular orbit speed, escape speed, and gravity at altitude around a simple spherical planet.
scenes.markdown:
# Orbit Basics: Speed vs Height

This scene introduces the three key quantities that control orbital insertion:

$$g(r)=\frac{\mu}{r^2}, \qquad v_{\text{circ}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}, \qquad v_{\text{esc}}=\sqrt{\frac{2\mu}{r}}$$

where:
- $r=R_p+h$ is distance from planet center (planet radius plus altitude),
- $\mu=GM$ is the gravitational parameter.

In the sliders, we use scaled values:
- $G_s = G\times 10^{11}$
- $M_x = M/10^{24}$
- so $\mu = G_s\,M_x\times10^4$ in km$^3$/s$^2$.

## What To Look For

1. As altitude $h$ increases, both $v_{\text{circ}}$ and $v_{\text{esc}}$ decrease.
2. Gravity weakens with $1/r^2$, but it never becomes zero.
3. Orbital insertion is mostly a **sideways speed problem**: you need enough tangential speed at the chosen altitude.

## Earth Reference (for context)

Using Earth-like values $R_p\approx 6371\,\text{km}$ and $\mu\approx 398600\,\text{km}^3/\text{s}^2$, low Earth orbit speeds are around $7.8\,\text{km/s}$ and escape speed near the surface is about $11.2\,\text{km/s}$.

These values match standard educational and agency references (NASA).
scenes.prompt: Teach orbital insertion as speed-height-energy tradeoffs. Emphasize that altitude alone does not make orbit; tangential speed is essential. Keep formulas tied to the live sliders.

views.name: Follow Spacecraft
views.description: Tracks the spacecraft — useful for watching forces as altitude changes

steps.title: Parameter 1: Planet Radius Rp
steps.description: Start with planet radius $R_p$. This sets the size of the world and the base geometric scale for orbit.

steps.title: Parameter 2: Orbit Altitude h
steps.description: Set orbit altitude $h$. The orbital radius is $r = R_p + h$. All speeds and forces in this scene are evaluated at this radius.

steps.title: Parameters 3-4: Gravity Inputs G and M
steps.description: Set gravity constant and planet mass with scaled sliders. We compute $\mu=GM$ live, then gravity from $g(r)=\mu/r^2$.

steps.title: Derived Targets: Orbital and Escape Speeds
steps.description: With $R_p,h,G,M$ fixed, the green arrow gives circular speed and the red arrow gives escape speed at the same radius.

steps.title: Force Balance: Gravity vs Centrifugal
steps.description: In the rotating-frame view, inward gravity and outward centrifugal balance at circular speed. Drag altitude to see both vectors shrink together with radius.

scenes.title: Passive Coast Flight (No Thrust)
scenes.description: This scene is a no-thrust coast model: choose initial speed and direction at launch height, then observe ballistic/gravity-dominated outcomes.
scenes.markdown:
# Launch and Insertion Simulator

This scene ties together **vehicle mass properties** and **orbital targets**.

## Important Modeling Note

This simplified simulator starts at a **launch-height point** at altitude $h$ (post-ascent), not from ground liftoff. So $h$ is the simulation start height, while $h_{target}$ is the desired orbit height.

## Mass and Propulsion Bookkeeping

- Dry mass: $m_d$
- Fuel mass: $m_f$
- Initial mass: $m_0=m_d+m_f$

Maximum ideal one-stage delta-v estimate:

$$\Delta v_{max}=I_{sp}g_0\ln\left(\frac{m_0}{m_d}\right)$$

(with $g_0=9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$).

## Gravity Inputs

We compute gravity live from:
- $G_s=G\times10^{11}$
- $M_x=M/10^{24}$
- $\mu=G_sM_x\times10^4$ (km$^3$/s$^2$)

## Target Speeds for Orbit

At launch-height radius $r=R_p+h$:

$$v_{\text{circ}}=\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}, \quad v_{\text{esc}}=\sqrt{\frac{2\mu}{r}}$$

If your speed is below circular, the trajectory is usually suborbital unless direction and staging are corrected.

## Pedagogical Model Used Here

For interactive clarity, this simulator uses a blended 2D model:
- ballistic-like arc when speed is below orbital target,
- near-circular motion around $v_{\text{circ}}$,
- outward escape branch near/above $v_{\text{esc}}$.

This keeps the controls intuitive while preserving the key speed-height logic used in real orbital mechanics.
scenes.prompt: Teach this as a mission design loop: (1) compute speed targets from height, (2) check delta-v budget from mass and Isp, (3) tune launch direction for insertion quality.

steps.title: Parameter 1: Planet Geometry and Gravity Inputs
steps.description: Set $R_p$, launch height $h$, and gravity inputs $G, M$. This defines $r=R_p+h$ and $\mu=GM$.

steps.title: Parameters 2-3: Dry Mass and Fuel Mass
steps.description: Now set vehicle mass. Total mass is $m_0=m_d+m_f$, and weight magnitude scales with $m_0g$.

steps.title: Parameter 4: Engine Efficiency (Isp)
steps.description: Side-by-side comparison: your selected-$I_{sp}$ vector first, with reference rocket baselines immediately next to it.

info.content: $$\frac{\partial \Delta v}{\partial I_{sp}}=\frac{g_0\ln(m_0/m_d)}{1000}={{toFixed(9.81*log((mdry+mfuel)/mdry)/1000,4)}}\,\text{km/s per s}\\ +10\,\text{s in }I_{sp}\Rightarrow {{toFixed(10*9.81*log((mdry+mfuel)/mdry),1)}}\,\text{m/s more }\Delta v$$

steps.title: Parameters 5-6: Launch Speed and Direction
steps.description: Set launch speed, flight-path angle, and target height. Green ring = launch height, red ring = target height.

info.content: {{vlaunch <= Isp*9.81*log((mdry+mfuel)/mdry)/1000 ? "Within ideal delta-v budget" : "Requested speed exceeds ideal one-stage budget"}}

steps.title: Parameters 7-8: Simulation Horizon and Playback
steps.description: Set the simulation horizon $T$, then animate playback with $\tau$ to inspect whether the trajectory falls, orbits, or escapes.

info.content: $$\text{Rule of thumb:}\;\phi\to 0^\circ\;\text{and}\;v\to v_{\mathrm{circ}}\;\text{for clean insertion at fixed }h.$$

scenes.title: Thrust-Driven Flight (Powered Model)
scenes.description: This scene adds sustained thrust and burn duration. Unlike passive coast, the rocket accelerates during burn, then coasts under gravity.
scenes.markdown:
# Thrust-Driven Flight (Powered Model)

This scene shows a simplified powered ascent/insertion model.

## Key Difference From Scene 2

- Scene 2: no sustained thrust after start (passive coast).
- Scene 3: thrust is applied for a burn duration $t_{burn}$.

## Simplified Powered Dynamics Used Here

We split time into burn and coast:

- $t_e = \min(t, t_{burn})$ (engine-on time)
- $t_c = \max(0, t - t_{burn})$ (coast time)

Radial and tangential motion get thrust contributions during burn, then coast with gravity.

This is still educational/simplified, but it captures the core operational idea: **orbit insertion depends on powered acceleration, not only an assumed initial speed**.
scenes.prompt: Emphasize that sustained thrust over burn time changes both speed and trajectory. Contrast this with no-thrust ballistic coast from scene 2.

functions.name: orbitExtremumT
functions.expr: (() => { let n = 720; let tBest = 0; let rBest = orbitR(0, mode); for (let i = 1; i <= n; i++) { let tt = i*T/n; if (orbitHit(tt, mode) > 0.5) break; let rr = orbitR(tt, mode); if ((isApo > 0.5 && rr > rBest) || (isApo <= 0.5 && rr < rBest)) { rBest = rr; tBest = tt; } } return tBest; })()

functions.name: orbitImpactT
functions.expr: (() => { let n = 720; for (let i = 0; i <= n; i++) { let tt = i*T/n; if (orbitHit(tt, mode) > 0.5) return tt; } return -1; })()

steps.title: Powered Flight Parameters and Playback
steps.description: Set launch/target heights, initial speed, thrust acceleration, burn time, and flight-path angle. Then animate with $\tau$.

info.content: Powered mode: thrust is active while $t\le t_{burn}$, then trajectory coasts under gravity.

scenes.title: Guided Two-Burn Orbital Insertion
scenes.description: A more realistic educational flight profile: ascent burn with pitch program, coast arc, then prograde circularization burn to achieve stable orbit.
scenes.markdown:
# Guided Two-Burn Orbital Insertion

This scene uses a simplified but physically grounded 2D orbital model with **central gravity** and **time integration**.

## Physics Model Used

State is propagated in the orbital plane with position and velocity:

$$\dot{\mathbf{r}}=\mathbf{v}, \qquad \dot{\mathbf{v}}=\mathbf{a}_g+\mathbf{a}_T$$

Gravity is not constant; it updates with altitude each step:

$$\mathbf{a}_g=-\mu\,\frac{\mathbf{r}}{\|\mathbf{r}\|^3}, \qquad g(r)=\frac{\mu}{r^2}$$

with

$$\mu=GM=(G_s\,M_x)\times10^4\;\text{km}^3/\text{s}^2$$

in this slider scaling.

## Circular-Orbit Balance

At radius $r$, the circular-orbit speed is

$$v_{\mathrm{circ}}(r)=\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{r}}$$

which is equivalent to centripetal balance:

$$\frac{v^2}{r}=\frac{\mu}{r^2}$$

So altitude alone is insufficient; you need the right **tangential speed** and near-zero radial speed.

## Flight Phases in This Scene

1. **Burn 1 (ascent + gravity turn):** thrust while pitch transitions from pitch_start to pitch_end over t_pitch.
2. **Coast:** no thrust for t_coast.
3. **Burn 2 (circularization):** guidance trims tangential speed toward $v_{\mathrm{circ,target}}$ and damps radial speed $v_r$.

## Numerical Integration Note

The trajectory is propagated with a stepwise symplectic-style update (interactive educational fidelity, not mission-grade guidance software).

## Stable-Orbit Criterion (Displayed Outcome)

After burns complete, the scene reports **stable-orbit target achieved** when all are met:

- $|r-r_{target}|\le 40\,\text{km}$
- $|v_\theta-v_{\mathrm{circ,target}}|\le 0.25\,\text{km/s}$
- $|v_r|\le 0.08\,\text{km/s}$

where $r_{target}=R_p+h_{target}$.

## Falcon 9-like Baseline (Simplified)

Defaults are tuned to approximate a Falcon 9 style ISS insertion in this educational model:

- $h_{target}=420\,\text{km}$ (ISS-class altitude)
- Burn 1 / coast / burn 2 sequence tuned for near-circular insertion

These are educational defaults, not mission-certified flight software parameters.
scenes.prompt: Explain the model transparently as a guided two-burn insertion under central gravity. Emphasize that g varies with radius (mu/r^2), show why circular orbit requires v^2/r = mu/r^2, and connect sliders to burn 1, coast, burn 2 guidance, and the explicit stability thresholds (radius error, tangential-speed error, radial speed). Contrast educational simplifications with real mission guidance.

functions.name: orbitExtremumT
functions.expr: (() => { let n = 720; let tBest = 0; let rBest = orbitR(0, mode); for (let i = 1; i <= n; i++) { let tt = i*T/n; if (orbitHit(tt, mode) > 0.5) break; let rr = orbitR(tt, mode); if ((isApo > 0.5 && rr > rBest) || (isApo <= 0.5 && rr < rBest)) { rBest = rr; tBest = tt; } } return tBest; })()

functions.name: orbitImpactT
functions.expr: (() => { let n = 720; for (let i = 0; i <= n; i++) { let tt = i*T/n; if (orbitHit(tt, mode) > 0.5) return tt; } return -1; })()

steps.title: Guided Ascent, Coast, and Circularization
steps.description: Tune two burns and pitch program to hit a stable orbit at target height.

info.content: Guided mode: burn 1 + gravity turn, coast, then prograde burn 2 for circularization.
